preamble: The phonetic transformation from a voiceless uvular plosive ([q]) to a voiceless alveolar plosive ([t]) primarily involves a shift in the place of articulation, moving the point of contact from the uvula at the back of the mouth to the alveolar ridge closer to the front. This process is known as fronting, where a sound produced further back in the vocal tract is replaced by one produced further forward. Such shifts can occur due to various phonological pressures, including simplification in language acquisition or dialectal evolution.
phoneticEffects: Fronting, change in place of articulation (uvular to alveolar)

examples.note: A documented phonological development in the Arbanasi dialect of Albanian involves the change of the uvular plosive /q/ to the alveolar plosive /t/. Specific word examples are not provided in the source, but the shift is explicitly stated as a regular development.
